long_candidates.reason: 4 politicians buying MSFT: Warner, Boozman, Fetterman, Mcguire
3 politicians buying JPM: Fetterman, Keating, Khanna

decisions.reasoning: Strong cluster of 4 politicians buying across both chambers (Warner, Boozman, Fetterman, McGuire), spread across a ~6-week window (Apr 3 – May 14). Warner sits on Senate Intelligence/Finance committees with meaningful tech oversight. Cross-partisan buying (both parties represented) reduces the chance this is coordinated reaction to one piece of public news. Cluster size of 4 is the strongest signal in this batch. Dollar amounts are small individually, but the breadth is what matters.
decisions.key_risk: Small individual trade sizes ($1K–$15K) suggest these could be routine diversified purchases rather than conviction bets on non-public information. Trades may also be reacting to publicly known AI/cloud tailwinds rather than privileged insight.

decisions.reasoning: 3-politician cluster buying JPM within a tight 6-day window (May 13–19), including Ro Khanna at a higher $15K–$50K tranche — the largest single amount in this dataset. Recency and tight clustering are positive signals. Financials/banking sector is directly overseen by Senate Banking and House Financial Services committees, adding some weight if any of these members sit on relevant committees.
decisions.key_risk: None of the three buyers are confirmed to sit on banking oversight committees. Fetterman, Keating, and Khanna are not primarily known as banking committee members. Without committee overlap, this is a weaker insider signal and may simply reflect macro optimism (e.g., rate environment, deregulation narrative).

decisions.reasoning: Only 2 politicians buying (Boozman and Fetterman), and Fetterman's buy is dated back to April 3 — over 5 weeks before Boozman's May 11 purchase, which dilutes the 'cluster' effect. Two buyers from the same chamber (both Senate) is a mildly positive signal, but below the threshold for a high-conviction entry. Big Tech is also under ongoing regulatory scrutiny which adds headline risk.
decisions.key_risk: Cluster is weak (only 2 actors, wide date spread). Regulatory/antitrust overhang on GOOGL remains a persistent risk. Insufficient signal to justify opening a new long position at this time.

decisions.reasoning: Two Senate-level politicians (Boozman and Warner) buying Constellation Energy within 7 days of each other (May 1–8). Warner sits on committees with energy/utility relevance. CEG is a nuclear energy play heavily tied to AI data center power demand and federal energy policy — exactly the kind of sector where Senate committee members could have informational edge. Cross-party buying (R+D) is a mild positive differentiator.
decisions.key_risk: Only 2 actors, small dollar amounts. Nuclear/energy policy can shift rapidly with regulatory decisions. CEG has already had a strong run on AI power demand narrative — much of the thesis may already be priced in publicly.

decisions.reasoning: Only 2 politicians buying (Warner and McGuire), one Senate and one House. AAPL is the most commonly held stock by politicians and retail investors alike, making it the hardest to distinguish insider-informed buying from routine portfolio allocation. No strong committee overlap signal identified. The cluster is minimal and the stock is universally covered — informational edge is lowest here.
decisions.key_risk: AAPL buys by politicians are extremely common and rarely signal anything beyond normal diversification. Signal-to-noise ratio is too low for a conviction entry. Tariff/supply chain risks (China manufacturing dependence) add further uncertainty.

decisions.reasoning: Only 2 politicians selling T (Fetterman and Gottheimer), and crucially Fetterman's sale was on April 3 — 47 days before Gottheimer's May 20 sale. This is NOT a tight cluster; the wide date gap significantly weakens the coordinated-selling thesis. Additionally, AT&T is a high-dividend telecom often sold for portfolio rebalancing or tax-loss purposes, not necessarily on negative non-public information. Short signals require higher conviction given unlimited downside risk.
decisions.key_risk: Shorting a high-dividend telecom with only 2 sellers, a 47-day gap between trades, and no confirmed committee oversight connection is insufficient conviction for a short. The trade date dispersion alone disqualifies this as a meaningful cluster. Risk of a short squeeze or dividend-driven support limits downside for the thesis.
